category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Numerical Analysis/Root Finding and Interpolation

questiontext.text: The bisection method applied to find a root in \([a,b]\) guarantees an error bound of \(\varepsilon_n\) after \(n\) steps given by

feedback.text: Correct. Each bisection step halves the interval, so after \(n\) steps the uncertainty is at most \((b-a)/2^n\).
Linear reduction in error would apply to the regula falsi method in ideal cases, not bisection; bisection gives exponential (geometric) reduction.
This would imply superexponential convergence, which is incorrect; bisection is only linearly convergent.

After \(n\) bisections, the interval has length \((b-a)/2^n\), so the error is bounded by half of that, i.e., \((b-a)/2^{n+1}\). While this tighter bound is also correct in some formulations, the standard statement is \((b-a)/2^n\).

questiontext.text: Newton-Raphson's iteration \(x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}\) converges with order of convergence

answer.text: Quadratic (order 2) near a simple root, meaning \(|e_{n+1}| \approx C|e_n|^2\) with \(C = |f''(\alpha)|/(2|f'(\alpha)|)\)

feedback.text: Correct. Taylor expansion of \(f(x_n)\) about the root \(\alpha\) shows the error satisfies \(e_{n+1} \approx \frac{f''(\alpha)}{2f'(\alpha)}e_n^2\), giving quadratic convergence for simple roots.
Newton-Raphson is quadratic, not linear, near simple roots; linear convergence would be too slow for most practical purposes.
Order 3 is the convergence rate of Halley's method, not Newton-Raphson.
Order \(\phi = (1+\sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618\) is the convergence rate of the secant method, not Newton-Raphson.

questiontext.text: The Lagrange interpolating polynomial through the nodes \((x_0,y_0),(x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_n,y_n)\) is \(P_n(x) = \sum_{k=0}^n y_k L_k(x)\). What is \(L_k(x_j)\)?

feedback.text: Correct. The cardinal polynomials \(L_k\) are constructed precisely so that \(L_k(x_j)=\delta_{kj}\), ensuring \(P_n(x_j)=y_j\) for all nodes.
If all basis values were 1, the interpolant would sum to \(\sum y_k\) at every node, not reproduce \(y_j\).
The basis polynomials \(L_k\) do not depend on the \(y\) values; they are functions of the nodes only.
No such relation holds; cardinal basis values are 0 or 1 at nodes, not proportional to \(k/n\).

questiontext.text: The error in polynomial interpolation of \(f \in C^{n+1}[a,b]\) at \(n+1\) distinct nodes \(x_0,\ldots,x_n\) is

feedback.text: Correct. This is the standard interpolation error formula, analogous to the remainder in Taylor's theorem, with \(\xi\) depending on \(x\).
The order of the derivative in the error formula is \(n+1\), not \(n\); and the factorial denominator is \((n+1)!\).
The error involves the product \(\prod(x-x_k)\), not the sum; and the factorial is \((n+1)!\).
This is a loose bound; the exact formula involves the node polynomial \(\omega_{n+1}(x)=\prod(x-x_k)\), which can be smaller or larger than \((x-a)^{n+1}\).

questiontext.text: The Newton divided-difference form of the interpolating polynomial is \(P_n(x) = f[x_0] + f[x_0,x_1](x-x_0) + \cdots + f[x_0,\ldots,x_n]\prod_{k=0}^{n-1}(x-x_k)\). The divided difference \(f[x_0,x_1]\) equals

feedback.text: Correct. The first-order divided difference is the standard slope formula; higher-order divided differences are defined recursively.
The divided difference \(f[x_0,x_1]\) approaches \(f'(x_0)\) as \(x_1 \to x_0\) (confluent case), but in general it is the finite difference quotient.
This is the arithmetic mean of function values, not the divided difference.
This is the forward difference \(\Delta f\), not the divided difference; the divided difference is divided by \(x_1 - x_0\).

questiontext.text: A natural cubic spline \(S(x)\) on \([a,b]\) with knots \(a=x_0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_n = b\) satisfies which conditions?

answer.text: \(S \in C^2[a,b]\), \(S|_{[x_k,x_{k+1}]}\) is cubic for each \(k\), \(S(x_k)=f(x_k)\) for all \(k\), and \(S''(a) = S''(b) = 0\) (natural BC)

feedback.text: Correct. Natural cubic splines have global \(C^2\) continuity, piecewise cubic form, data interpolation at nodes, and vanishing second derivatives at the endpoints.
A \(C^3\) piecewise cubic on \(n\) intervals would be globally a single cubic; splines are only \(C^2\), not \(C^3\).
Natural (or "free") boundary conditions for splines impose \(S''(a)=S''(b)=0\), not zero first derivatives (which would be "clamped" conditions).

answer.text: \(S\) is a polynomial of degree at most 4 on all of \([a,b]\)

feedback.text: A cubic spline is piecewise cubic, not a single degree-4 polynomial; the piecewise structure is essential to the definition.

questiontext.text: The fixed-point iteration \(x_{n+1} = g(x_n)\) converges to a fixed point \(\alpha = g(\alpha)\) if, on an interval containing \(\alpha\),

answer.text: \(|g'(x)| \leq L < 1\) for some Lipschitz constant \(L\), and \(g\) maps the interval into itself

feedback.text: Correct. The Banach fixed-point theorem requires \(g\) to be a contraction (\(|g'|\leq L < 1\)) on a closed interval it maps into itself, guaranteeing a unique fixed point and linear convergence.
If \(g'(\alpha)=0\) the iteration converges quadratically locally, but this is not the general condition for convergence.
This would make \(g\) an expansion, not a contraction, and the iteration would diverge.
When \(|g'(\alpha)| = 1\) the iteration may fail to converge; the contraction condition requires \(|g'| < 1\) strictly.

questiontext.text: The secant method uses the recurrence \(x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)\frac{x_n - x_{n-1}}{f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})}\). Its order of convergence near a simple root is

feedback.text: Correct. The secant method converges with order equal to the golden ratio \(\phi=(1+\sqrt5)/2\), which is the positive root of \(p^2=p+1\) arising from the error recurrence.
The secant method avoids computing \(f'\) but pays a price: order \(\phi\approx1.618\) is less than quadratic.
Linear order 1 would be too slow; the secant method achieves superlinear order \(\approx1.618\).
Order 3 is the convergence rate of Halley's method; the secant method has order \(\phi\approx1.618\).

questiontext.text: Runge's phenomenon refers to the behavior of polynomial interpolation of \(f(x) = \frac{1}{1+25x^2}\) on \([-1,1]\) using equally spaced nodes as the degree \(n \to \infty\). The interpolating polynomial \(P_n\)

answer.text: Oscillates with increasing amplitude near the endpoints, so \(\|P_n - f\|_\infty \to \infty\) for equally spaced nodes

feedback.text: Correct. With equally spaced nodes the Lebesgue constant grows exponentially, leading to divergent oscillations near \(\pm 1\) (Runge's phenomenon). Using Chebyshev nodes avoids this.
Uniform convergence fails for equally spaced nodes with Runge's function; this is the content of Runge's counterexample.

answer.text: Converges in \(L^2\) but diverges pointwise at interior points

feedback.text: The divergence in Runge's phenomenon is uniform norm divergence near the endpoints, not just pointwise interior divergence.

answer.text: Converges only at the interpolation nodes \(x_k\) and diverges everywhere else

feedback.text: By construction \(P_n(x_k)=f(x_k)\) always; the divergence in Runge's phenomenon is near the endpoints between nodes.

questiontext.text: To minimize the maximum interpolation error \(\|f - P_n\|_\infty\) over all choices of \(n+1\) nodes in \([-1,1]\), the optimal nodes are the Chebyshev nodes \(x_k = \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2(n+1)}\right)\), \(k=0,\ldots,n\). The resulting minimum of \(\max_{x\in[-1,1]}|\omega_{n+1}(x)|\) where \(\omega_{n+1}(x)=\prod_{k=0}^n(x-x_k)\) equals

feedback.text: Correct. The monic Chebyshev polynomial \(\tilde{T}_{n+1}(x) = T_{n+1}(x)/2^n\) has minimum sup-norm \(1/2^n\) among all monic degree-\((n+1)\) polynomials, so Chebyshev nodes minimize \(\|\omega_{n+1}\|_\infty\).
The minimum sup-norm is \(1/2^n\), not \(1/n!\); the exponential decay in \(n\) is base 2, not factorial.
The minimum is \(1/2^n\), which decays exponentially; \(2/(n+1)\) decays only algebraically.
No extra \(\pi\) factor arises; the minimum is exactly \(1/2^n\).

questiontext.text: Gauss-Legendre quadrature with \(n\) nodes is exact for polynomials of degree at most

feedback.text: Correct. With \(n\) nodes and \(n\) weights (total \(2n\) free parameters), Gauss-Legendre quadrature achieves exactness for all polynomials up to degree \(2n-1\).
Exactness for degree \(n\) is what Newton-Cotes with \(n+1\) points achieves; Gauss quadrature does much better.
Degree \(n+1\) would be the exactness of the trapezoidal rule with appropriate nodes, not Gauss quadrature.
Gauss quadrature is exact for degree \(2n-1\), not \(2n\); it cannot integrate \(x^{2n}\) exactly with \(n\) nodes.

questiontext.text: The composite trapezoidal rule for \(\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\) with \(n\) equal subintervals of width \(h=(b-a)/n\) has a global truncation error of order

answer.text: \(O(h^2)\): the error is \(-\frac{(b-a)}{12}h^2 f''(\xi)\) for some \(\xi \in (a,b)\)

feedback.text: Correct. The composite trapezoidal rule has error \(O(h^2)\); the leading term involves \(f''\) integrated over \([a,b]\), giving \(-\frac{b-a}{12}h^2f''(\xi)\).
Order \(h^4\) is the error of composite Simpson's rule, not the trapezoidal rule.
The trapezoidal rule achieves second-order accuracy (\(O(h^2)\)); first-order would be the midpoint or left-endpoint rectangle rules without symmetry.
Order \(h^3\) arises for the single-interval trapezoidal rule (local error), but the composite rule's global error is \(O(h^2)\).

questiontext.text: Simpson's \(\frac{1}{3}\)-rule for integrating \(f\) over \([a,b]\) using a single panel (with midpoint \(m=(a+b)/2\)) gives

feedback.text: Correct. Simpson's rule uses the quadratic interpolant through \(a, m, b\); the error involves the fourth derivative and is \(O(h^5)\) per panel (global \(O(h^4)\)).
This is the trapezoidal rule, not Simpson's rule.
This is Simpson's \(3/8\)-rule with three equal subintervals, not the standard \(1/3\)-rule.
This is the midpoint rule; Simpson's rule also uses the endpoint values with weight 1 each and midpoint with weight 4.

questiontext.text: Gaussian elimination without pivoting applied to \(A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}\) produces an \(LU\) factorization \(A = LU\). The matrix \(L\) is

answer.text: Unit lower triangular (1s on diagonal, multipliers \(m_{ij} = a_{ij}^{(k)}/a_{jj}^{(k)}\) below the diagonal)

feedback.text: Correct. The lower triangular factor \(L\) has 1s on the main diagonal and the elimination multipliers below; \(U\) is the resulting upper triangular matrix.
An orthogonal factor arises in QR decomposition, not LU; Gaussian elimination produces triangular factors.

answer.text: Diagonal with entries equal to the pivots of \(A\)

feedback.text: The diagonal matrix of pivots appears in the \(LDU\) decomposition; in \(LU\), \(L\) is lower triangular (not diagonal) and \(U\) carries the pivots.

answer.text: Upper triangular with diagonal entries equal to 1

feedback.text: \(L\) is lower triangular (not upper triangular) with 1s on the diagonal; \(U\) is upper triangular.

questiontext.text: Euler's method for \(y' = f(x,y)\), \(y(x_0)=y_0\) with step size \(h\) gives \(y_{n+1} = y_n + hf(x_n,y_n)\). The local truncation error is of order

answer.text: \(O(h^2)\) (local), giving a global error of \(O(h)\) — Euler's method is first-order accurate

feedback.text: Correct. The local truncation error (error per step) is \(\frac{h^2}{2}y''(\xi)\) (\(O(h^2)\)), but accumulated over \(O(1/h)\) steps the global error is \(O(h)\).
Second-order accuracy describes the modified Euler (Heun) or RK2 methods, not the basic Euler method.
Euler's method does converge; the local error is \(O(h^2)\) and global error \(O(h)\).
This describes the classical 4th-order Runge-Kutta method, not Euler's method.

questiontext.text: The classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta method for \(y'=f(x,y)\) uses four stage evaluations. The correct formula for the update \(y_{n+1}\) is

feedback.text: Correct. This is the classical RK4 formula; the weights \(1,2,2,1\) (summing to 6) give fourth-order accuracy with local truncation error \(O(h^5)\).
This is a third-order method (Kutta's \(3/8\)-rule variant); classical RK4 has four stages with weights \(1,2,2,1\).
This is the Heun (modified Euler) method — a second-order Runge-Kutta method with two stages, not fourth-order.
This is the midpoint (RK2) method — second-order with two function evaluations, not fourth-order RK4.

questiontext.text: The Gauss-Seidel iterative method for solving \(A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}\) is guaranteed to converge for any initial guess if

answer.text: \(A\) is strictly diagonally dominant: \(|a_{ii}| > \sum_{j \neq i}|a_{ij}|\) for all \(i\)

feedback.text: Correct. Strict diagonal dominance guarantees convergence of both Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel iterations; for positive definite symmetric matrices, Gauss-Seidel also converges.
Symmetry alone does not guarantee convergence of Gauss-Seidel; a symmetric indefinite matrix can lead to divergence.
Positive diagonal entries are necessary but not sufficient; strict diagonal dominance is the standard sufficient condition.
Invertibility and positive determinant do not imply convergence of iterative methods; the spectral radius condition on the iteration matrix must hold.

questiontext.text: The power method for finding the dominant eigenvalue of a matrix \(A\) (the one with largest absolute value) converges with rate governed by

answer.text: \(\left|\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right|^k\) where \(|\lambda_1| > |\lambda_2| \geq \cdots\); convergence is faster when this ratio is small

feedback.text: Correct. The power method error after \(k\) iterations is \(O(|\lambda_2/\lambda_1|^k)\); the method stalls when \(|\lambda_2| \approx |\lambda_1|\) (nearly equal dominant eigenvalues).

answer.text: \(1/k\) (algebraic decay regardless of the eigenvalue gap)

feedback.text: The power method converges geometrically (exponentially fast), not algebraically; the rate depends on the eigenvalue ratio.
The convergence rate depends on the ratio \(|\lambda_2/\lambda_1|\), not the difference \(|\lambda_1|-|\lambda_2|\).

answer.text: The method always converges in exactly \(n\) steps for an \(n\times n\) matrix

feedback.text: The power method is an iterative method that converges asymptotically; it does not terminate in a finite number of steps in general.

questiontext.text: The condition number \(\kappa(A) = \|A\|\cdot\|A^{-1}\|\) of a matrix governs the sensitivity of the linear system \(A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}\). If \(\kappa(A) = 10^6\) and the relative error in \(\mathbf{b}\) is \(10^{-10}\), the relative error in the solution \(\mathbf{x}\) satisfies

feedback.text: Correct. The standard perturbation bound gives relative error in solution \(\leq \kappa(A) \times\) relative error in data; with \(\kappa=10^6\) and relative perturbation \(10^{-10}\), the bound is \(10^{-4}\).
Machine epsilon (\(\approx 10^{-16}\)) bounds floating-point rounding, not the amplification of data errors; the bound here is \(10^{-4}\), much larger.
A large condition number amplifies errors; the relative error in \(\mathbf{x}\) is up to \(\kappa(A)\) times larger than in \(\mathbf{b}\).
The condition number multiplies (amplifies) errors; dividing by \(\kappa\) would imply the system is a contraction, which is the opposite of ill-conditioning.

questiontext.text: The QR algorithm for finding all eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix \(A\) proceeds by computing \(A = Q_0R_0\) and setting \(A_1 = R_0Q_0\), then iterating. This iteration preserves which property?

answer.text: The eigenvalues of \(A_k\) are the same as those of \(A\) (the matrices are orthogonally similar: \(A_{k+1} = Q_k^T A_k Q_k\))

feedback.text: Correct. Each QR step is an orthogonal similarity transformation, so eigenvalues are preserved; the sequence \(A_k\) converges to an upper triangular (or diagonal for symmetric) matrix revealing the eigenvalues.

answer.text: The characteristic polynomial of \(A_k\) changes at each step, but the trace is preserved

feedback.text: The characteristic polynomial is fully preserved (all eigenvalues are preserved), not just the trace.
Orthogonal similarity preserves the Frobenius norm (\(\|A_k\|_F = \|A\|_F\) for all \(k\)); it does not decrease to zero.
Orthogonal similarity also preserves the determinant; \(\det(A_k) = \det(A)\) for all \(k\).
